speaker: interviewer
text: With Lola Snow. It's the 10th of November, 1995. So Lola, you and Walter went to France.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which part of France did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Paris first. And the ship was supposed to go in June or a few days after June. We lived very nicely in natural, because it's only a few days that we stayed in Paris. But the ship got delayed and delayed from day to day. And it happened that I got pregnant in Paris. But the pregnancy didn't last. And I started bleeding. And  the doctor said that it's now time for curette. I should stay in bed, and it will be all right. And I was already maybe four or five months-- four, maybe four pregnant when they had to take it away, the baby. Because the-- the ship was going, I think, on the 15th of September from Marseilles. And from that day on that I had the curette, I started to suffer with nerves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you became very depressed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Depressed. The whole body was hurting me. I had heart palpitation. I had pains everywhere for years and years and years. I could have had a year good and 18 months terrible. And that's how my life was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this-- this period of being well and unwell in this depression lasted for a very long time with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: A very long time-- most of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so this was like a thing that happened as a reaction after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it, because I became-- I started to feel very guilty that I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that feeling of being guilty.

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt guilty that on so many times I was supposed to be called and to be dead. And somehow, I-- I managed-- I left everybody else, and I survived myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so that-- that--

speaker: interviewee
text: That feeling. I didn't know why. Why should I survive and everybody should be dead? And that guilt, I have still today a guilt feeling. Whatever happens, anything happens in the family, that I had nothing to do with it, I straight away excuse myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't-- you didn't find any comfort in the fact that you had survived and that the thought that maybe you were meant to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did eventually after I saw

speaker: interviewer
text: After you saw that movie, that changed your attitude?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I thought that I should've been dead for so and so many years. So most probably, I had a legacy to survive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I built, again, a family. I have got a wonderful daughter and a beautiful, wonderful son. And we have a lovely relationship with each other. And I have five grandchildren, all [NON-ENGLISH] all right. And I have to be thankful for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was that the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have a lovely daughter-in-law and a lovely son-in-law. And with all of them, I have a good relationship.

speaker: interviewer
text: And up until the time you saw that movie,

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt guilty.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that movie, seeing that, changed everything for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it made you--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what? There are many happy events in my life. But with the happy events, I always used to get sick. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't feel well on my son's bar mitzvah. I'll show you the album. And you will think that I am a film star. But I didn't feel well. I didn't feel well on my daughter's wedding, or in between, or something, even my son's wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it took you years and years and years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Years and year Until today, I have to take a tablet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just to-- just to get over this depression.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I take one every night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to France. So you got on the ship.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got on the ship. We got on the ship. The ship-- the condition of the ship was similar to the concentration camp. [LAUGHTER] Because we lived three-- three flights down in barracks men separately, women separately. It was terribly hot. It was set there. We traveled for 10 weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a what?

speaker: interviewee
text: de [INAUDIBLE] It was a French boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: A French, so it had a French crew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. French for horrible. But nobody cared. And we-- we traveled for 10 weeks. Nobody slept in their bed in there. We all slept on the deck.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was so hot.

speaker: interviewee
text: So hot. And we traveled 10 weeks. We seen the world, really from Martinique, to Panama, to Tahiti, to New Hebrides, New Caledonia. We've seen everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the French territories.

speaker: interviewee
text: All different, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you arrive in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 25th of November, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's almost your anniversary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We celebrated our 40th with the people that arrived. And now soon, we can celebrate the 50th.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you arrived in Australia, tell me about coming here. Tell me about that period.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came here to my husband's sister and her family. They waited for us on the boat. And they took us home. And they, you know, they were happy to have us. But I-- we had a room in a hotel not far from where they lived. My sister in law had a delicatessen shop. And I straight away started to work with her to help her without language, without nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the deli? Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Neutral Bay.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Neutral Bay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at the times were-- you know, my husband wanted to become a hawker. And he had the license already to become a hawker. And then our friend, Mr. [INAUDIBLE], came and said, look, you don't know the language. Come and work for a few weeks. And then you do what you want. And he stayed with Mr. [INAUDIBLE] for 28 years. And I started to-- I started to work at the delicatessen shops. I worked very hard. And I stopped only when I became pregnant with Sandra.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was Sandra born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sandra was born October 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: And once she was born, did you stop work? Or, did you continue working?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I stopped work. I stopped work while-- till my son was-- when-- I-- I-- I was working practically all the time. But when the kids were little, I wasn't working. And if I was working, I-- I-- I tried always to be home when they were home from school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when was your son born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son was born on the 3rd of February, 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you mentioned this depression that continued with you. Did you get any help for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am telling you, I was lying on the couch with a-- with a psychologist for two years. And he never asked me about concentration camps, nothing. I was telling him about my father, about what happened today, what happened yesterday. But he never said, what were you doing during the war? And that's why my-- that's why I never got better.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had no concept of what you had been through.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. When I applied for the [NON-ENGLISH] here.

speaker: interviewer
text: The reparations.

speaker: interviewee
text: The restitution money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my-- and I-- I I submitted also the-- the bills from that psychologist. They didn't even want to look at it, because they said he had no license to-- to be a psychologist in Australia. He was one of the-- of the-- students of Freud.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was someone who had come-- was it an Australian-born person or a European-born person?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, a European-born person. Dr. Stark was his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he had no--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. He asked me-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Never dealt with that period in your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most probably, he didn't-- he-- he didn't know anything about this. And I never talked about it, because I couldn't talk. You ask my children. I never talked to them about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as your children were growing up--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you-- you went back to work. And you worked a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked also in the delicatessen as well. When my son went to school, I remember I took him to school when he was five. And I cried, because I saw that I have no babies anymore. And I tried to get a job somewhere. So I got a part-time job in Winyah Delicatessen. I worked very hard there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all this time you were feeling very down and unwell.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes I didn't know even how I'm going to go approach the next customer. But I did. And I carried on a normal life at home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had good-- sometimes a couple of hours good ones, too. But sometimes I had-- no-- only when I fell asleep. And it was hard for me to fall asleep. But I looked well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you really had to come through this by yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you had to pull yourself through.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or, the tablets.

speaker: interviewer
text: And between the two--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the end, it-- it has come all right for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your-- your children. What do they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was always my pride, because she was such a beautiful girl and such a nice girl, you know. She-- she never was any trouble. Trouble, the only trouble I had was to push her to study.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Otherwise, I had no troubles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does your son do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son wanted always to be a good boy. Am I a good boy, mommy? Am I a good boy? And still today people remember him. And he was always a good boy. He never gave me a minute worry in his life. I tried to do the best I could for them. I sent them to the best school. I sent Sandra to SCEGGS, where I didn't know where I'm-- how I was going to pay the next term. But I did. And Gary was supposed to go to [INAUDIBLE]. I enrolled him when he was born to [INAUDIBLE]. But he had to pass the exam. And he didn't pass it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went to [INAUDIBLE]. But he is a-- he is quite successful in his way. He is today a pharmacist and also very much in-- in-- in with the computers. He has got his own firm of computers. So you have seen your children grow up here and flourish. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do wonderfully.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Sandra has two beautiful boys-- Darren and Justin. And my son has got one son, Nathan, who is still at school, and two girls, which are-- Nathan is at [INAUDIBLE]. And the two girls are at Canberra-- Nicole and Danielle. Nicole is 13. And Daniella is 8.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have your whole family--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --here and around you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Around you. They found live in eastern suburbs. So we see each other quite often.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you-- when you look back on the-- on the incredible experience that you went through--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --do you have any feelings of why it was you who managed to come through when so many people didn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I'm telling you, that that was the feeling of guilt.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't feel there was luck as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I at times, I felt that was my destiny. Not so much luck as destiny. I was destined. My destination was to survive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't feel maybe luck. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you know what? Somehow, you have to help your luck. And you have to help your destiny. If I wouldn't have gone to that authority stand and said that I want to go on my own free will to the camp, I wouldn't be here, would I? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If I would have stayed in Lvov in the ghetto, I wouldn't be here, would I? So you believe that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was meant to--

speaker: interviewer
text: And that [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because most probably, I was meant to bring up a family again to make the Jewish nation grow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, when you-- when you look back on what has happened to you, is there a message? Is there something that you feel you want to say to the world about those events and those nice times?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, there is always a message that I do hope that none of the future generation and after generation of todays will never experience the misery the-- of life, the fright, the-- the humiliation which I went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Lola, I want to thank you very much for sharing this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I hope-- I hope that because I suffered so much that in the way, I gave my children a way of life that they can be happy. And they can build their family in-- in-- in the right way and have a much better and much more fruitful life than I had. But I am quite happy with my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Lola, thank you for allowing us to share part of it with you. Lola, can you introduce this gentleman to us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband of 50 years, Walter. He was--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my lovely wife. Day I-- where I won the lottery.

speaker: interviewer
text: The day you won the lottery?

speaker: interviewee
text: I won the lottery 50 years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's true.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm grateful that I met him and that we built a beautiful family together. And we celebrated now our 50th wedding anniversary together with our family.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what can I say? We had our ups and downs as everybody. But in an overall, we're a happy couple.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, may you be for many, many more years to come.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We hope. We hope.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at the same time, I wanted to thank Mr. Spielberg that let us-- he let me talk about what was hidden in me for so many years. I think this is a special therapy for me. I hope it  helps me to go on with my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, Walter. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Chava Szejndla [INAUDIBLE] Majerczyk.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this photo  taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it must have been taken in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: In one of the spas. It may be in [INAUDIBLE], Krynica I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, who is here?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my only brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Benjamin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me when these pictures were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: One picture on the left was taken in 1921, and on the right, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one in 1921, would that have been a school picture, school photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, both are school photos. Both I took them-- I enlarged them from a school photo, from a group photo.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. Your brother and your sister-in-law?

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my brother and my sister-in-law. Her name was Lola, too. And that's on their honeymoon in 1934.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where that was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Gdynia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is in which country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Lola, who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my nephew, my brother's son. He was born on the 2nd of July, 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name-- we called him [INAUDIBLE], but his name was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the little boy who was with your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, what do we have here?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have a class. I don't remember in 1937 or 1938-- a class-- there's our-- our teacher of-- German teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The one standing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That one.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the glasses? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The only-- older man.

speaker: interviewer
text: The only adult.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, adult, older man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are in the front row.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was at the high school, the gymnasium.

speaker: interviewee
text: The gymnasium, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I don't remember '36 or '37, '38, '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went with the-- with the gymnastic teacher to Zakopane, which was the best place to go for skiing in Poland in the mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this--

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have been here 13 or 14 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was a school skiing trip.

speaker: interviewee
text: A school skiing trip.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this is a picture made in Bedzin. I think it was after my father died, because I can see it-- see that  I wear here black stockings. And I'm here together with one of my best friends, [INAUDIBLE], who is my friend still today.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this picture would've been taken after--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1938, Maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, after 1937. OK, thanks. I find it  so extraordinary. You're not rolling are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, tell me about this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: That photo was taken on the first birthday of my husband that we celebrated together. It was about three, four months after we got married on the 20th of October 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my Sandra.

speaker: interviewer
text: Taken when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think taken on her second birthday, which must be October, 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: And taken in which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my gorgeous Gary.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was Gary here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gary was here maybe one, one and  a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this would be which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It would be maybe the end of '53.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's taken in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Sydney. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in front of our home in 45 Murray Street, Bronte. And it was taken when Gary was 7 and Sandra 10. We were coming back from a bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the year would be?

speaker: interviewee
text: There, '59.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, who are the young gentleman here?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are my Sandra's sons-- Darren and Justin. They gave me lots of joy in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are-- are they here in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they'd be here about four and a half and two and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the picture would've been taken around when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Around 1975, '76.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, who have we got here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my darling Gary with his three offsprings, Danielle in the bathtub about a few months old, Nikki holding on to her, and also Nathan. They are three gorgeous kids-- bright and gave me lots of [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: When would this have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Danielle is eight now. So she was only a few months old, about a eight years ago or seven and three quarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, tell me about the picture here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think looking at this picture, there is a reason why I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is here?

speaker: interviewee
text: There is here Gary and Judy, my daughter-in-law with their three children. They're really a lovely couple. They live for each other. And they  are very happy together. Behind them is Victor and Sandra and Darren and Justin. Victor and Sandra have been married for nearly 25 years. And they are liked and-- and adored by many people. And they give us lots of joy. And we're very grateful to our destiny that let us have this beautiful family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
